narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength and determination it takes to get here. Your profile shows a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.80, which is a bit below the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset, along with Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 4 and 3 respectively—lower than the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5—while your family comes from a high income bracket and you plan to attend full-time, like most applicants. The model's prediction for you is a 55% probability of failing the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the scales just enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass with high confidence. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 26, well below the dataset average of 36.7, which had the strongest influence in signaling higher risk to the model. On a more positive note, your high family income stood out as a major counterbalance, strongly pulling the prediction toward success by suggesting resources that often help students thrive. Your undergraduate GPA also helped in this direction, nudging things toward a pass despite being slightly under average. Smaller influences included your lower Year 3 decile slightly favoring failure, while your full-time status and Year 1 decile offered minor boosts toward passing. Compared to typical students who get approved, your LSAT was the key gap—most successful ones score much higher—while your family's financial strength actually positions you better than many in that regard. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman leader in law; it's one model's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, perhaps with targeted prep that leverages your high family support, and strengthen your academic deciles through additional coursework or recommendations. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing that core area—keep pushing, you've got this. We're rooting for you.
